articles.title: Daily AI Briefing — Friday, June 12, 2026 ET
articles.summary:
Daily AI Briefing — Friday, June 12, 2026 ET

1. Today’s actual signal
- GitHub Agentic Workflows entered public preview: Markdown-described agent automations compile into standard GitHub Actions, with read-only defaults, sandboxing, safe-output validation, and threat detection. Source: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-11-github-agentic-workflows-is-now-in-public-preview — credible because it’s GitHub Changelog; materially new vs yesterday’s “agent logs/security” theme.
- GitHub Agentic Workflows can now use Actions’ built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` instead of long-lived PATs; org runs bill AI credits to the org, with cost controls recommended. Source: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-11-agentic-workflows-no-longer-need-a-personal-access-token — credible because it’s GitHub Changelog; pricing/spend risk exists.
- OpenCode `v1.17.4` shipped today with practical agent plumbing: `cwd` for local MCP servers, connector-based auth, stored provider credentials, v2 session APIs, sticky `X-Session-Id`, and visible errors for content-filtered model responses. Source: https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/releases — credible because it’s the official GitHub release page for an open-source coding agent.

2. Dustin relevance
- Hermes/agents: GitHub is validating “agent workflows as auditable CI jobs,” not just chat sessions. Useful pattern: Markdown instruction → compiled runner workflow → sandbox/permissions/logs/cost cap.
- Fanbase Weather: No strong signal today.
- Newsletter/Data Nerve: Angle is getting sharper: agents are moving into boring infrastructure — Actions, tokens, sandboxes, cost caps, logs — because that’s where trust lives.
- App/product ideas: A cheap “agent workflow receipt” layer could summarize: trigger, prompt/instructions, permissions, files touched, tests run, token/cost estimate, human approval status. Start local/open-source; avoid paid GitHub AI credits until validated.

3. Try / Save / Ignore
- Try today: Spend 20 minutes reading GitHub’s Agentic Workflows quickstart/docs from the changelog and write one private “would I trust this?” note: trigger, permissions, cost cap, rollback, human approval.
- Save for later: OpenCode’s `cwd` support for local MCP servers — useful pattern for repo-specific Hermes tools without global config mess. Source: https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/releases
- Ignore: “fully autonomous SDLC” marketing. The useful part is smaller: agent tasks inside controlled runners with receipts.

4. Content angles
- “The agent future is less ‘AI coworker’ and more ‘weird intern inside GitHub Actions with a helmet, a budget, and logs.’”
- “Trustworthy agents are becoming infrastructure: tokens, sandboxes, cost caps, approvals, receipts.”

5. One next action
- Draft one 6-line private template called “Agent workflow receipt”: task, trigger, permissions, files changed, validation run, approval needed. No build, no posting, no config change.

Verification: sources checked, repetition check performed against recent local cron outputs, no external side effects.

articles.title: Development application raises new questions in Cave City data center debate
articles.summary: Glasgow News 1 is reporting on Development application raises new questions in Cave City data center debate. In plain English, this is a large-load planning story: the important details are whether data centers are driving new power demand, who pays for the grid upgrades, and how regulators or lawmakers protect ordinary ratepayers. For Dustin, the useful read is whether this changes the local utility story around capacity, reliability, rates, regulation, metering, or the data products leaders need to explain those tradeoffs clearly. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
articles.emailSummary: A data-center story with possible implications for power demand, grid upgrades, and who pays for capacity.
articles.whyItMatters: This is close to Dustin’s utility analytics world: it may affect metering, reliability, customer impact, rate context, regulatory reporting, or the data products leaders need to trust.

articles.title: EPIC: Regulations will decide if electric customers will pay for data centers' costs
